## Performance Evaluation

This section reports the held-out performance of the latest completed run of
the thesis experiment. The aim is not just to list metrics, but to judge
whether the TD3 agent's behavior remains economically coherent once returns,
turnover, and path-dependent risk measures are inspected together. Results are
loaded from exported thesis snapshots, with a local tracking-store fallback, so
that the rendered chapter stays tied to recorded evaluation artifacts.

```{python}
#| label: perf_eval_setup
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from PIL import Image

from thesis_mlflow_results import load_experiment_snapshot, format_key_metrics

EXPERIMENT_NAME = "TRADINGENV_TD3_AAPL_HFT_STATE_SPACE"
snapshot = load_experiment_snapshot(EXPERIMENT_NAME)
finished = snapshot.latest_finished
```

### Selected Metrics from the Latest Completed Run

```{python}
#| echo: false

# Try real data first
key_metrics_df = None
if finished is not None:
    key_metrics_df = format_key_metrics(finished.get("evaluation_report"))
    if key_metrics_df.empty:
        key_metrics_df = None

# Fall back to mock data matching format_key_metrics() schema
if key_metrics_df is None or key_metrics_df.empty:
    print("Placeholder values shown — run full experiment pipeline to populate.\n")
    key_metrics_df = pd.DataFrame([
        ("Total Return", "0.28%"),
        ("CAGR", "22.36%"),
        ("Annualized Volatility", "12.34%"),
        ("Sharpe Ratio", "1.8234"),
        ("Sortino Ratio", "2.4123"),
        ("Max Drawdown", "-0.16%"),
        ("Win Rate", "56.21%"),
        ("Profit Factor", "1.3845"),
        ("VaR (95%)", "-0.0021"),
        ("CVaR (95%)", "-0.0034"),
    ], columns=["Metric", "Value"])

display(key_metrics_df)
```

### Benchmark Comparison

The TD3 agent is evaluated against five benchmark strategies spanning passive exposure, execution-style references, and a stochastic baseline. The table below summarizes key risk-adjusted metrics for each strategy, with the agent's performance highlighted in the first row.

```{python}
#| echo: false

# Try real data first
benchmark_df = None
if finished is not None:
    benchmark_report = finished.get("benchmark_report")
    if benchmark_report and "benchmark_comparison_table" in benchmark_report:
        benchmark_df = pd.DataFrame(benchmark_report["benchmark_comparison_table"])

# Fall back to mock data matching benchmark_comparison_table schema
if benchmark_df is None or benchmark_df.empty:
    print("Placeholder values shown — run full experiment pipeline to populate.\n")
    benchmark_df = pd.DataFrame([
        ("TD3 Agent", 0.0028, 0.2236, 0.1234, 1.8234, 2.4123, -0.0016, 0.5621, 0.64),
        ("Buy & Hold", -0.0012, -0.0980, 0.1440, -0.6800, -0.8100, -0.0034, None, 0.00),
        ("Short & Hold", 0.0012, 0.0980, 0.1440, 0.6800, 0.9100, -0.0022, None, 0.00),
        ("TWAP", -0.0008, -0.0650, 0.1480, -0.4400, -0.5200, -0.0029, None, 0.02),
        ("VWAP", -0.0007, -0.0570, 0.1500, -0.3800, -0.4500, -0.0027, None, 0.02),
        ("Random", 0.0001, 0.0080, 0.1680, 0.0500, 0.0600, -0.0043, 0.5010, 0.98),
    ], columns=[
        "Strategy",
        "Total Return",
        "Annualized Return (CAGR)",
        "Annualized Volatility",
        "Sharpe Ratio",
        "Sortino Ratio",
        "Max Drawdown",
        "Win Rate",
        "Turnover",
    ])

# Format numeric columns for display
for col in benchmark_df.columns:
    if col == "Strategy":
        continue
    if col == "Win Rate" or col == "Sharpe Ratio" or col == "Sortino Ratio":
        benchmark_df[col] = benchmark_df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x*100:.2f}%" if pd.notna(x) else "N/A")
    elif col == "Turnover":
        benchmark_df[col] = benchmark_df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.2f}" if pd.notna(x) else "N/A")
    else:
        benchmark_df[col] = benchmark_df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.4f}" if pd.notna(x) else "N/A")

display(benchmark_df)
```

### Evaluation Metadata Snapshot

```{python}
#| echo: false

if finished is not None:
    params = finished.get("params", {})
    latest = finished.get("latest_metrics")
    rows = [
        ("Run Name", finished.get("run_name")),
        ("Status", finished.get("status")),
        ("Start Time", str(finished.get("start_time"))),
        ("End Time", str(finished.get("end_time"))),
        ("Seed", params.get("seed")),
        ("Train Size", params.get("data_train_size")),
        ("Algorithm", params.get("training_algorithm")),
        ("Reward Type", params.get("env_reward_type")),
        ("Training Steps (latest metric)", None if latest is None else latest.get("training_steps")),
        ("Final Reward (latest metric)", None if latest is None else latest.get("final_reward")),
    ]
    meta_df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Field", "Value"])
    display(meta_df)
```

### Evaluation Plots

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

if finished is None:
    print("No finished run available to display evaluation plots.")
else:
    plots = finished.get("evaluation_plots", {})
    ordered = [
        ("Merged comparison", "merged_comparison"),
        ("Actual returns", "actual_returns"),
        ("Rewards", "rewards"),
        ("Positions", "positions"),
    ]

    for title, key in ordered:
        plot_path = plots.get(key)
        if plot_path and Path(plot_path).exists():
            img = Image.open(plot_path)
            fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
            ax.imshow(img)
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.set_title(title)
            plt.show()
        else:
            print(f"{title}: artifact not found")
```

### Discussion: Reading Performance in the Context of State Design

The key interpretive issue in this thesis is not whether the agent attains a
single high metric, but whether the reported behavior is consistent with the
microstructure-aware state design proposed in Chapter 4. Because the current
work studies order-book-derived state representations, performance should be
read jointly with turnover, drawdown, and action behavior rather than through
return metrics alone.

This matters especially when comparing simpler snapshot-heavy feature sets with
broader state representations that also encode flow, timing, and spread-regime
information. A richer state is only useful if it improves held-out behavior in
an economically credible way. If additional features increase turnover, produce
fragile policy behavior, or improve reward diagnostics without improving net
returns, they do not strengthen the empirical case for the proposed state
design.

For that reason, the performance tables in this section should be interpreted as
evidence about the quality of the overall decision pipeline: state
representation, reward specification, and policy learning together. The central
question is whether the broader microstructure-aware feature design yields more
stable and more interpretable out-of-sample behavior than simpler alternatives,
not whether any single metric is maximized in isolation.
